segments.s068-g89: سپاهی is the noun 'troops/army' (subject). که introduces a relative clause ('whom/that'), so the clause that follows qualifies سپاهی. سگسار is an epithet placed inside the relative clause and functions as what others call those troops. خوانندشان is a classical-present form (3rd person plural) 'they call them' (خوانند + شان enclitic 'them'); the verb ends the relative clause as is normal in Persian.
segments.s068-g90: پلنگان is the noun 'leopards' used here metaphorically ('warlike/lethal fighters'); جنگی is a postpositive adjective 'warlike' or 'of war' modifying پلنگان; نمایندشان is the classical-present 3rd plural 'they make/show them (to be)' with the object pronoun -شان attached to the verb, so the clause says 'they portray/make them war‑leopards.' The verb form is the same shape as modern present but used narratively in the epic.
segments.s068-g91: ز من is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'from me' or 'about me.' چون here introduces the temporal/causal clause ('when/when suddenly'), and بدیشان is 'to them' (بدی-شان = به + ایشان in archaic form). رسید is the past simple 'reached/arrived' and آگهی is the noun 'news/information' (object): together 'when news about me reached them.'
segments.s068-g92: از آواز من gives the cause/ground 'from/of my voice' or 'at the sound/reputation of me.' مغزشان is the noun مغز 'brain/mind' plus the possessive -شان 'their.' شد تهی is the two-word predicate 'became empty' (شد = 'became'; تهی = 'empty'), a stative result meaning 'their minds went blank' or 'they were stunned.'
segments.s068-g93: به + شهر + اندرون forms a prepositional phrase 'into/within the inner part of the city' (به ... اندرون = toward/into the inner quarters). نعره برداشتند is a verbal phrase where برداشتند (past, 3rd‑person plural) combines with نعره to mean 'they raised/let out a cry' — here برداشتند is used in the sense 'took up/raised' rather than a literal 'took away'. Word order is topical (place phrase before the verb) as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s068-g94: ازان پس is a temporal connective 'after that' (ازان = 'from/that' + پس = 'after/then'). همه شهر بگذاشتند is a clause with همه 'everyone' as subject, شهر as the object/place, and بگذاشتند (past, 3rd‑person plural) here meaning 'they left/abandoned' (i.e. they forsook the city). The verb follows the subject as usual in Persian.
segments.s068-g95: همه 'everyone' is the subject; پیش من is a prepositional phrase 'in my presence' (پیش = 'before/in front of' + من). جنگ جوی is a nominal phrase where جنگ ('battle/fight') is modified by جوی (here adjectival/participial 'seeking' or 'desirous of'), so 'war‑seeking' or 'eager for battle'. آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came.' The order is Subject — (prepositional phrase) — object/description — Verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s068-g96: چنان ('so'/'thus') modifies the following adjectives. خیره here means 'staring' or 'astonished/entranced' (a state of stunned fixation). The repeated پوی پوی is a poetic reduplication: پوی ('swift') repeated to stress rapid, eager movement; together they intensify manner ('very swift' or 'rushing one after another'). آمدند is again the past plural 'they came.' The clause describes the manner of their coming.
segments.s068-g73: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. شاه جهاندار is a doubled royal title (شاه = 'king', جهاندار = 'sovereign'), with the noun-first Persian order. بگشاد روی is a poetic compound: بگشاد (past of گشودن with the intensive/poetic prefix بـ) = 'revealed/opened', and روی = 'face' — together meaning 'revealed his countenance/showed his face.'
segments.s068-g74: زمین را ببوسید is a simple past clause: زمین = 'the ground/earth' with را marking it as a definite direct object, and ببوسید = 'he kissed.' The conjunction و links the action to شد پیش اوی, where شد is the past of شدن 'became/happened' used here to mean 'came/approached' and پیش اوی = 'before him/in his presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of', اوی = third-person pronoun).
segments.s068-g75: منوچهر is the proper name 'Manuchehr.' برخاست is the past simple 'rose/stood up.' از تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'from the ivory throne' (از = 'from', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory' functioning as a qualificative noun/adjective after تخت in classical style).
segments.s068-g76: ز یاقوت رخشنده is a genitive/qualifying phrase introduced by poetic ز (= 'of' / 'from'), here meaning 'of (radiant) ruby' (یاقوت = 'ruby', رخشنده = 'radiant'). بر سرش تاج is the locative phrase 'a crown on his head' (بر = 'on', سرش = 'his head'), with the whole verbless clause appended to the prior action — a common epic construction where the verb 'to be' is omitted and the crown is presented as placed on his head.
segments.s068-g77: بر خویش بر تخت is a doubled prepositional phrase emphasizing possession and place: بر = 'on/upon', خویش = 'his own' (possessive reflexive), بر تخت = 'on the throne.' بنشاختش is a past transitive form (بـ + نشاختن) with a third-person object enclitic -ش referring back to the possessed thing/person; together the clause functions as 'he set/seated himself (or him) upon his throne' — a typical classical idiom where the object marker enclitic co-refers to the possessive phrase.
segments.s068-g78: چنان چون سزا بود is a fixed formula meaning 'as was fitting' or 'in the manner proper' (چنان / چون = emphatic 'such/as', سزا = 'desert/what is due', بود = past copula 'was'). بنواختش is a past form of نوختن with enclitic -ش and here has a poetic/idiomatic sense 'dealt with it / treated it accordingly' (the verb is literary and can mean 'struck/handled/treated'); the -ش again refers back to the previously mentioned object/situation.
segments.s068-g79: وزان is a compound/poetic connector meaning 'and from that/from them,' used to introduce a list of descendants or retinues. گرگ‌ساران is a plural agent noun 'wolf-keepers/wolf-hunters' and جنگاوران is the plural 'warriors' — the two nouns are coordinated as groups coming from the preceding subject.
segments.s068-g80: Again وزان = 'and from them.' نره is an adjective 'male' qualifying the following noun. دیوان here denotes the supernatural beings (classical 'divs' / demons) though the supplied lemma senses list lacks that meaning; مازندران is the place-name 'Mazandaran', so the phrase means 'the male divs/demons of Mazandaran.' Grammatically, the adjective precedes the noun and the place-name identifies their origin/realm.
segments.s068-g57: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and sits before the noun phrase. بارگاه is a royal court/palace; منوچهر شاه is the name plus title in apposition (name + ‘king’), so the whole phrase means 'toward the court of Manuchehr, the king.'
segments.s068-g58: به فرمان او is an instrumental phrase 'by/at his command' (به + فرمان + pronoun او). برگرفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural compound verb (بر + گرفتند) meaning 'they took up/they set out' and راه (placed after the verb) is the path/road they took — together 'they took the road.'
segments.s068-g59: چون ... یافت is a temporal clause 'when he found/learned'; ز (poetic از) + the following pronoun indicate 'of it' or 'from it' and آگهی means 'news/information' — together: 'when Manuchehr learned the news of it.'
segments.s068-g60: بیاراست is a past, 3rd-person singular verb meaning 'he adorned/arrayed/prepared.' دیهیم here refers to the rural people or peasantry (used as a noun), and شاهنشهی is an adjective 'imperial' (or 'of the king of kings') modifying that group — 'he arrayed the rural levies in imperial style.'
segments.s068-g61: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing source places. ساری و آمل are two place names joined by و ('and'). برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the noun 'roar/uproar' — literally 'from Sari and Amol a roar arose.'
segments.s068-g62: چو here means 'like/as' introducing a simile. دریای سبز is 'a green sea' (metaphor). اندر آمد is a compound 'came in / surged inward' and به جوش 'into boiling' expresses the manner (it 'surged boilingly' or 'came in with a boil').
segments.s068-g63: ببستند is a past 3rd-person plural 'they fastened/they bound.' آیین here means 'manner/custom/arrangement' and ژوپین is the gambeson or padded jacket; together ژوپین‌وران denotes those wearing such coats (the warriors wearing gambesons). The phrase conveys that they secured their protective jackets.
segments.s068-g64: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/they went forth.' با 'with' introduces an accompaniment or instrument; خشتهای is the plural of خشت ('mudbricks' or bricks) and گران ('heavy' or 'valuable') modifies it — 'they went off with heavy bricks.'
segments.s068-g129: چو آمد به نزدیک من سرفراز is a temporal clause: چو (when) + آمد (came) introduces the circumstance. به نزدیک من = 'near me/toward me' (preposition phrase after the verb, as is common in Persian). سرفراز is an epithet placed after the pronoun phrase and names the approaching person ('the proud/victorious one').
segments.s068-g130: من از چرمه چنگال کردم دراز literally: 'I, from the leather, made/extended a claw long.' Here کردم is simple past 'I did/made' and functions with دراز (postpositive adjective) to mean 'I extended/made long' the chengāl (claw/gauntlet finger) that came out of the leather (چرمه = glove/skin). The agent 'من' is explicit; از چرمه marks the source ('out of my leather/glove').
segments.s068-g131: گرفتم is past first-person 'I seized/took'; کمربند مرد دلیر is the direct object in the order 'belt of the brave man' (noun + genitive modifier). The adjective دلیر follows the noun مرد and qualifies it ('the brave/valiant man').
segments.s068-g132: ز زین برگسستم is a continuation of the action: ز (from) زین (the saddle) + برگسستم (I dislodged/removed/broke away) — a 1st-person past verb showing the speaker unseated the man. بکردار شیر follows as an appositive description: 'of lion-like bearing' or 'a lion in his conduct' (بکردار = 'of conduct/character' used adjectivally, شیر = 'lion' as epithet).
segments.s068-g133: زدم is past 'I struck/struck down.' بر زمین = 'onto the ground.' چو + پیل ژیان is a simile: چو = 'like/as' and پیل ژیان = 'a living elephant' (پیل = elephant, ژیان = living/animate), so the blow is compared to the force/weight of a living elephant.
segments.s068-g134: بدین آهنین دست و گردی میان is descriptive: بدین (= 'with this / by this means' in poetic usage) + آهنین دست = 'iron hand' (an epithet for a strong, iron-like blow). و گردی میان = 'and a cloud/whirl of dust in between' (گردی = dust, میان = between/in the middle). Together they qualify how the strike landed.
segments.s068-g135: چو افگنده شد شاه زین گونه خوار is another temporal clause: چو (when) + افگنده شد (was cast/thrown down — افگنده is a past participle used with شد to form 'was thrown') + شاه (the king). زین گونه خوار is a comment on his condition: زین = 'from the saddle' (or 'from/at the saddle'), گونه = 'in this manner' and خوار = 'humiliated/despised', so the clause reads 'when the king was thrown down from the saddle and thus humbled.'
segments.s068-g136: سپه روی برگشت از کارزار: سپه = 'the army/host' (subject), روی برگشت = 'turned back/retreated' (روی 'face/turn' used metaphorically + برگشت 'returned/turned'), and از کارزار = 'from the battlefield.' The verb برگشت is simple past/stative here describing the army's retreat.
segments.s068-g97: سپَه (subject) + a reduplicated verbal phrase جنب جنبان شد expresses vigorous motion: جنب + جنبان intensifies 'to move/stir', and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was' that completes the predicate. The coordinated short clause و روز تار ellipses the verb (شد) and means 'and the day grew dark' (تار used poetically for 'dark'). Word order is: subject → predicate (compound verbal phrase).
segments.s068-g98: پس is a temporal/consecutive adverb 'then'. اندر فراز آمد is a motion clause: اندر 'into/on/within' + فراز 'height/top' + آمد 'came'. The conjunction و links a final locative phrase پیش غار where پیش means 'in front of / before (the presence of)' and غار is 'cave'. Together: 'then they came up onto the height and before the cave.'
segments.s068-g99: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: نبیره 'scion/descendant' + جهاندار (title 'world‑ruler') + سلم (proper name) + بزرگ 'great' as a postpositive adjective. Persian allows the possessor/title to follow the head noun, so this reads 'the scion of the world‑ruler Salm the Great.'
segments.s068-g100: به پیش سپاه اندر آمد is a motion clause with a goal: به پیش 'to the front/in the presence of' + سپاه 'army' + اندر آمد 'came in/entered'. The simile چو گرگ (چو = 'like/as' + گرگ 'wolf') modifies the manner of coming. Word order: prepositional phrase (goal) → verb → simile.
segments.s068-g101: سپاهی is the plural/group noun 'soldiers' or 'the army'. به کردار introduces manner ('in the manner of' or 'like'), where کردار refers to behavior/conduct. مور و ملخ are the comparative images 'ants and locusts' describing their swarming. So the whole NP reads 'soldiers, like ants and locusts in their manner.'
segments.s068-g102: نبد is an archaic negative-past copula equivalent to 'was not' (cf. نبود). It governs the predicate دشت پیدا 'plain visible' (دشت = plain, پیدا = visible). The following correlative negation نه کوه و نه شخ 'neither mountain nor ___' continues the list of things not visible. This is standard Persian negative coordination with نه ... نه.
segments.s068-g103: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. برخاست is the past 'rose/departed'. زان 'from that/of that' links to the previous referent 'that army' (لشکر). گشن (hungry) + گرد forms a resultative 'turned/had become hungry' where گرد is a verbal element meaning 'to become/turn' in this idiomatic pairing.
segments.s068-g104: رخ نامداران ما is a genitive construction 'the face(s) of our famed/renowned ones' (رخ = face/appearance; نامداران = the illustrious). گشت is past 'became/turned' and زرد is the resultant adjective 'yellow/pale' (here 'pale'). The order is possessor phrase → verbal predicate → adjective result.
segments.s068-g121: گمانم (my thought / my suspicion) is a first-person possessive noun used to mean 'I supposed' here. چنان بد که is a sequence meaning 'so/that (so bad) that...', where چنان is an intensifier and که introduces the result clause. سندان سرش literally 'the anvil of his head' places the two nouns in the Persian ezāfe-like compound order (sindan 'anvil' + sar-e-sh 'his head'), i.e. 'his head like an anvil.'
segments.s068-g122: که شد دوخته is a result clause: که 'that' + شد used as an auxiliary/past marker 'became/was' + دوخته 'sewn' (past participle). مغز تا مغفرش uses تا 'as far as / up to' to connect مغز 'brain' with مغفرش 'his covering/cap' (مغفر + possessive -ش), i.e. 'the brain sewn up to his covering.'
segments.s068-g123: نگه کردم is the verb phrase 'I watched / I kept watch' (نگه 'look/watch' + کردم auxiliary). از گرد here means 'from around / nearby' (گرد = around), and چون پیل مست is a simile 'like a drunken elephant' (چون = like, پیل = elephant, مست = drunk/intoxicated, used figuratively).
segments.s068-g124: برآمد is a simple past intransitive 'arose / sprang up / emerged.' یکی here means 'a certain one / someone' (indefinite), تیغ هندی 'an Indian blade' is a noun phrase (تیغ = sword/blade, هندی = Indian), and به دست is a short locution meaning 'in the hand / with (in) hand' (به used here as a prepositional marker and دست 'hand').
segments.s068-g125: چنان آمدم is 'I came thus / in that manner' (چنان = thus/so, آمدم = I came). شهریارا is a vocative 'O king' (the poet addresses the king), and گمان here restates the speaker's suspicion/expectation ('I suspected / my thought was'). Note the somewhat elliptical word order typical of poetry.
segments.s068-g126: کزو = 'from which / from whom' links the following noun phrase to the previous suspicion. کوه 'mountain' is used metaphorically; زنهار here is a noun 'warning / admonition' (not the interjection). خواهد is the future/auxiliary 'will' and بجان 'at the cost of life / to my life' expresses the severity (i.e. 'would cost my life' or 'threaten my life').
segments.s068-g127: وی اندر شتاب و من اندر درنگ contrasts the two parties: وی 'he' + اندر شتاب 'in haste' versus من 'I' + اندر درنگ 'in delay/hesitation.' The preposition اندر ('in') precedes the nouns to form locative phrases; the verse uses parallel structure.
segments.s068-g128: همی جستمش is a continuous imperfective sense 'I kept seeking/trying for it' (همی = continually/still, جستمش = I sought it). تا کی آید = 'until when it should come' (تا = until, کی = when, آید = come). به چنگ = 'into (my) claw/grasp' (به = to/into, چنگ = claw/talon, here figurative for 'grasp').
segments.s068-g65: سپاهی is a collective noun 'an army/soldiers'; که is a relative pronoun introducing the following descriptive clause. از ... تا ... is the standard 'from ... to ...' range expression, here repeated with کوه 'mountain' for emphasis. مرد follows to name the members ('men') of that army; the clause is a nominal relative clause describing سپاهی rather than a full finite sentence.
segments.s068-g66: سپر در سپر is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'shield by shield' or 'shield to shield' (i.e., tightly arrayed). ساخته is the past participle of ساختن used adjectivally/predicatively ('made/arranged'), and سرخ و زرد are color adjectives attached to the preceding noun phrase (the shields), placed after it in poetic word order.
segments.s068-g67: ابا here functions as an archaic/spelling variant of با 'with' (instrumental), introducing a list of instruments. کوس 'kūṣ' and نای 'ney' are instruments; رویین follows نای as a postpositive adjective meaning 'made of metal/iron' ('metal ney'). سنج is another percussion instrument (cymbals) in context. The coordinating و links the items; word order places modifiers after the noun in poetic style.
segments.s068-g68: Again ابا = با 'with' (instrumental/possessing) introduces another list. تازی modifies اسپان to mean 'Tāzī horses' i.e. Arabian horses; اسپان is the plural 'horses'. پیلان (plural of پیل) 'elephants' and گنج 'treasure' continue the enumeration. The sequence is a simple coordinated list joined by و.
segments.s068-g69: ازین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' (از + این with the orthographic ین), modifying the clause. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host' and پذیره شدند is a passive/compound expression 'were received/accepted/arrayed' (پذیره as noun/adjective plus شدند 'they became/they were'). The overall meaning: 'the host was received/arrayed in this fashion.'
segments.s068-g70: بسی is an adverb meaning 'many/much' or 'in great number' (an emphatic/quantifying word). با introduces the means 'with banners (درفش) and tabīreh' (تبیره here denotes standards/inscriptions/explanatory devices in context). شدند is the past plural 'they were/made' — together: 'they were set up/arrayed with banners and tabīreh.'
segments.s068-g71: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when'); آمد is simple past 'came'. به نزدیکی بارگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity of the royal court/palace' serving as the complement of آمد.
segments.s068-g72: پیاده شد literally 'he became on-foot' i.e. 'he dismounted / went on foot' (past tense). The clause و راه بگشاد شاه shows inverted/orderly poetic syntax: راه 'the way' (object) + بگشاد (past form 'opened') + شاه (subject) — meaning 'and the king opened the way.' بگشاد is the past of گشودن used here as a simple past ('opened').
segments.s068-g17: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). تاب گیرد is a two-word verb phrase where تاب (here: 'turn/motion, sway') is the thing that 'takes' (گیرد, present subjunctive/optative of گرفتن used in a conditional sense). سوی مادرش is a prepositional phrase 'toward his/her/its mother' with the possessive enclitic -ش attached to مادر; the whole clause means 'if it turns toward its mother'.
segments.s068-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. گفت here functions as a noun 'the utterance / saying' (not a full finite verb), followed by the verbal predicate پراگنده گردد ('will become scattered' — پراگنده as predicate adjective and گردد, present subjunctive/passive-like form of شدن/گشت used to mean 'become' or 'be scattered'). سرش ('his/its head') is the noun + enclitic object/possessive. Word order places the verb (گردد) at the end, typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s068-g19: کند is the finite verb 'does/makes' (here future/gnomic sense). شهر ایران is the direct object 'the city of Iran' (noun + genitival place name). پر آشوب is a compound where پر ('full of') qualifies آشوب ('turmoil'), so پر آشوب means 'full of turmoil'; the coordinating و links آشوب with رنج ('suffering'). The overall phrase describes what he causes: 'he makes the city of Iran full of turmoil and suffering.'
segments.s068-g20: بدو is the short prepositional/enclitic form 'to him / unto it'. بازگردد is the present/subjunctive form of بازگشت(ن)/بازگردانیدن 'to return', here impersonal/anticipatory 'will return'. مگر introduces an exception 'except / nothing but'. تاج و گنج list the exceptions 'the crown and the treasure'. The clause therefore means 'to him will return nothing except the crown and the treasure.'
segments.s068-g21: همه موبدان is the subject 'all the mōbeds (Zoroastrian priests)'. آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation / praise' (something they utter). خواندند is the past plural verb 'they recited/sang/voiced' — in poetic narrative it often means they uttered or chanted the phrase. The clause therefore says that all the priests gave acclamation.
segments.s068-g22: ورا is the postpositional/oblique pronoun 'to him'. خسرو here functions as the name/title 'Khosrow' (proper name). پاک‌دین is an honorific compound 'pure of faith / devout', attached as part of the name/title. خواندند is the same past plural verb form as above but here used with the sense 'they called (him) [by that name]' — 'they named him Khosrow Pak-din.'
segments.s068-g23: بگفتند is the past tense 'they said'. کز is the poetic relative/connector 'that' (introducing reported speech). ما 'we' is the group being compared and تو 'you' is the addressee; داناتری is the comparative 'more knowing / wiser'. The clause reports their words: 'they said that among us you are the wiser.'
segments.s068-g24: به here means 'in/with regard to' or 'therefore/for this reason' (linking consequence). بایستها is the modal sense 'ought / should' (the dictionary entry is the deverbal 'must/ought'); بر in this comparative/contextual use points over/above (i.e., 'more than'), and تواناتری is the comparative 'more able / more powerful'. The short clause expresses the expected consequence: 'you should be the more capable.'
segments.s068-g9: چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). ایران is the direct object of the verb in the next segment ('I brought Iran'); ز + چنگال شیر و پلنگ means 'from the claws of the lion and the leopard' (ز = 'from' with the following noun phrase). Word order is temporal marker + object + prepositional phrase denoting separation.
segments.s068-g10: برون is an adverb meaning 'out/forth' and combines with آوریدم (past, 1st person 'I brought') to form 'I brought out'. The two repeated به phrases are instrumental ('by/through')—به رای 'by counsel' and به جنگ 'by war/battle'—listing the means used. آوریدم is an older/past form ('I brought').
segments.s068-g11: فریدون is the subject (proper name). ز ضحاک marks what is being removed/cleansed ('from Zahhak'); گیتی ('the world') is the object of بشست. بشست is a past tense verb meaning 'washed'/'cleansed' (he washed the world of Zahhak).
segments.s068-g12: بترسم is an expression 'I fear' (imperfective/present sense used to introduce a subordinate clause). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'. آید is a present/subjunctive-like 3rd person 'comes'/'will come'. ازان تخم رست = 'from that seed (تخم) will sprout/grow' (رست = sprout/grow). The clause expresses worry that something will arise from that seed.
segments.s068-g13: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / must not' and که introduces the clause that follows. بر خیره literally 'upon/before the خیره'—خیره here means those who stare or the astonished onlookers, so بر خیره = 'in the presence of the gawkers'. از عشق زال means 'out of/because of love for Zal' (از = 'because of' here). Word order: impersonal modal + that-clause headed by the locative phrase and the cause phrase.
segments.s068-g14: همال ('porter, carrier') is the noun of the clause; سرافگنده is an adjective 'ashamed/abashed'; گردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive of شدن used here as 'becomes' or 'may become'. The final همال repeats/addressing the porter (vocative/emphatic repetition). So: 'the porter becomes ashamed — O porter.'
segments.s068-g15: چو is the temporal 'when'. از دخت مهراب = 'from the daughter of Mehrab' (دخت = daughter; مهراب here is a proper name in context, not the architectural term). و از پور سام = 'and from the son/descendant of Sam' (پور = son/descendant; سام is a proper name). This clause lists parentage/origins that trigger what follows.
segments.s068-g16: برآید is a 3rd-person verb 'arises/appears/comes forth'. یکی qualifies تیغ ('one single' sword). تیغ تیز = 'sharp sword' (noun + adjective). از نیام = 'from the scabbard' (نیام = scabbard); word order is verb + numeral + noun + adjective + prepositional phrase.
segments.s068-g113: بیامد is a past-tense verb ('came') placed at the start (verb-first inversion common in epic poetry). به نزدیک من is a prepositional phrase ('near me' / 'toward my vicinity') where به + نزدیک + من gives the locative 'close to me'. جنگ ساز is the nominal phrase identifying who came: a compound where ساز functions as 'maker/one who causes' (poetic use), 'war-maker' or 'warrior'.
segments.s068-g114: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'). پیل ژیان is a poetic compound: پیل 'elephant' with ژیان giving an emphatic/living quality (here 'mighty/elephant-like'); the adjective/epithet follows the noun in this older poetic order. با کمند دراز is an instrumental/attendant phrase ('with a long lasso'), with دراز placed after کمند as the postpositive adjective.
segments.s068-g115: مرا is the object ('me') formed by م + را (object marker) placed before the verb for emphasis. خواست is the past of خواستن here meaning 'aimed for' or 'sought' (an inanimate subject targeting someone). به خم کمند is a locative phrase 'at the bend/curve of the lasso' (به + خم + کمند).
segments.s068-g116: چو دیدم is a temporal subordinate clause ('when I saw'), with چو = 'when' and دیدم = 'I saw'. خمیدم is the main past verb 'I bent/stooped' (action taken). ز راه گزند uses poetic ز (= از 'from') + راه گزند literally 'from the path of harm', idiomatically 'to avoid injury'.
segments.s068-g117: کمان کیانی is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian (royal) bow' (modifier follows or is attached as an epithet). گرفتم is the past verb 'I took/seized' and appears after the object (SOV order typical). به چنگ is an adverbial phrase 'into my grip / in my hand' (به + چنگ, with چنگ used figuratively as 'hand/grip').
segments.s068-g118: به introduces additional items taken or instruments ('with' / 'along with'); پیکان پولاد is 'an arrow of steel' with the adjective پولاد placed after the noun, and و تیر خدنگ links 'and a shaft/arrow that is like a dagger' (تیر modified by خدنگ). The whole segment enumerates the projectiles/secondary weapons taken.
segments.s068-g119: عقاب تکاور is a compound nominal: عقاب 'eagle' qualified by تکاور ('bold/raider' as an epithet), forming the thing roused. برانگیختم is the first-person past 'I roused/let loose' and follows the object in poetic word order.
segments.s068-g120: چو آتش is a simile ('like fire'). بدو = به + او/it here functions as 'at him/at it' (direction/target). بر تبر uses بر meaning 'against/upon' with تبر 'axe' as the target/context, and ریختم is the past of ریختن used in the sense 'I threw/poured' — together: 'like fire I hurled it at him against the axe.'
segments.s068-g105: من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. این گرز is an instrumental/identifying phrase 'this mace' placed before the clause. یک زخم برداشتم uses the past tense برداشتم (برداشتن) in 1st-person singular; the idiom زخم برداشتن here means 'to receive/take a wound'. Word order places the instrument and the wound before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s068-g106: سپه را marks 'the army' with the definite/object marker را (showing the army as the affected party). هم is the adverb 'also'. آنجای (آنجا) is a locative adverb 'there'. بگذاشتم is the past tense of گذاشتن with the poetic prefix ب-, meaning 'I left/left behind' (I put/left the army at that place). The clause therefore means 'I also left the army there.'
segments.s068-g107: خروشی is the noun 'a roar/clamor' and خروشیدم is the past 1st-person verb 'I roared/uttered a cry' (خروشیدن). از پشت زین is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the saddle' giving the source/location of the cry. The noun + verb repetition (خروشی خروشیدم) is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s068-g108: که introduces a result/relative clause here ('so that' / 'such that'). چون is the simile 'as/like'. آسیا شد is 'became (like) a mill' (شد as past of شدن). بریشان is بر + ایشان contracted, meaning 'over/against them' (literally 'upon them'). زمین is the subject 'the earth/ground' — the poetic order puts result (ground becoming like a mill) after the simile.
segments.s068-g109: دل آمد is an idiom 'a heart/courage came' i.e. 'courage returned.' سپه را uses the object/affected-marker را to indicate the army is the entity that is affected (literally 'the heart came to the army' → 'the army regained heart'). همه بازجای combines همه 'all/everyone' with بازجای 'back to their place/again in place', indicating everyone resumed their positions.
segments.s068-g110: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely'. سوی رزم is a directional phrase 'toward battle' (سوی = toward, رزم = battle). کردند رای is the verb کردن in past 3rd-plural with رای as its object: رای کردن = 'to deliberate/resolve', so کردند رای = 'they resolved/decided'. The whole clause means 'throughout they set their minds on battle / they resolved for battle.'
segments.s068-g111: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past 3rd-singular 'he heard'. کاکوی functions as the subject 'the caller' or 'the one who cries/calls' (poetically, could be a bird or herald). آواز من is the object 'my voice/call' (آواز + possessive من). Word order here is verb–subject–object, a poetic inversion.
segments.s068-g112: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'such/so'. زخم is 'a wound'. سرباز کوپال من is a nominal phrase 'the soldier Kūpāl of mine' — note classical Persian order: noun (سرباز) + proper name (کوپال) + possessor (من) = 'my soldier Kūpāl.' The clause is elliptical/poetic about a wound befalling that soldier.
segments.s068-g81: بپرسید is an archaic/past third‑person form meaning “he asked” (the preverb ب‑ here is part of classical past formation). و = “and.” بسیار تیمار خورد is a small clause meaning “received/took great care/attention”: بسیار modifies تیمار (“much care”), and خورد is past (here ‘to befall/receive’/get) making تیمار the thing received. Word order places the verb (خورد) at the end of the clause.
segments.s068-g82: سپهبد is the subject/head noun “the spahbed (general).” سخن is the object “speech/words.” یک به یک is an adverbial phrase “one by one” (به here helps form the per‑item expression), and یاد کرد is a compound verbal expression: یاد (‘‘remembrance/mention’’) + کرد (past of کردن) = “recalled/related/recited.” Word order is subject–object–adverbial–verb with کرد as the light/main perfective verb.
segments.s068-g83: که introduces a subordinate/clause (“that/so that”). نوشه here denotes a draught/nectar (a figurative ‘drink’), and زی‌ای is the vocative/optative structure with زی (imperative/optative “live”) plus ای (vocative) addressing شاه (“O king”). تا جاودان is a temporal/adverbial phrase “until/for eternity.” Together this clause is an optative/blessing: “that, O king, you live/drink until eternity.”
segments.s068-g84: ز is the poetic preposition “from/concerning.” جان تو is the genitivestructure “your life.” کوته بد بدگمان is an elliptic/nominal clause: کوته (short/brief) and بد (bad/ill) qualify بدگمان (suspicion/distrust). The verb that would make it a wish or curse is omitted; the intended sense is optative/fulsome: “may ill suspicion be short with respect to your life” (i.e. may suspicion not linger over your life). Word order is compact and elliptical, with the copula/optative verb left implicit.
segments.s068-g85: برفتم is a past first‑person verb “I went/set out” (classical prefix بر‑ + رفتن), with the verb in final position in the clause. بران = بر آن (“to that”) functioning as a locative/directional phrase; شهر is the noun “city”; دیوان is the genitive/attributive noun (here ‘Divs’/‘demons’ in the epic sense) and نر qualifies it (نر = ‘male’, here an epithet). The noun phrase شهر دیوان نر = “that city of the (male) Divs.” Note: some listed lemma senses for برفتم/دیوان did not match the epic senses, so sense indices reflect that.
segments.s068-g86: نه is the negative/emphatic “not” (introducing a contrast). دیوان is the noun being negated (“Divs”); که functions as a conjunction introducing the corrective clause (“but/for”). شیران here is used metaphorically for ‘brave men/warriors’ (literally ‘lions’), جنگی is an adjective “warlike/battle‑worthy,” and به بر is a locative/adverbial phrase meaning “at their side/with them” (به + بر, ‘on/upon’). The clause implies an omitted copular verb: “[they are] not Divs, but warlike lions are with them.”
segments.s068-g87: که introduces an explanatory/comparative clause (“that/for”). از is the comparative preposition “than/from.” تازی qualifies اسپان: تازی اسپان = “Arabian horses/cavalry.” تکاور‌ترند is a comparative adjectival predicate: تکاور (daring) + ـ‌ترند (they are more …) = “they are bolder/more daring.” The verb is present/plural (“are more daring”).
segments.s068-g88: ز again marks the comparative “than.” گردان ایران = “the troop/host of Iran” (گردان = troop/unit, ایران = Iran). دلاور‌ترند is the comparative predicate formed from دلاور (“valiant”) + ـ‌ترند, meaning “are more valiant/braver.” Together this clause states the second comparative: “[their force] is braver than the Iranian host.”
segments.s068-g33: هم آنگاه is a temporal phrase meaning 'right then' or 'at that moment' (هم here works as an intensifier). برخاست is a simple past (3rd person) meaning 'rose' or 'stood up'. فرزند شاه is a noun phrase 'the son of the king' placed after the verb (poetic word order: temporal phrase + verb + subject).
segments.s068-g34: سرنهاده به راه is a verbal participial construction from سرنهادن به راه, an idiom meaning 'having set out on the road' or 'set off' (سرنهاده is a past participle). ابا ویژگان here appears as a name/epithet placed before the participle; به marks direction toward the way. In other words: '[Name/epithet] having set out on the road.'
segments.s068-g35: سوی ... نهادند روی is an idiom: روی نهادن به/سوی = 'to turn one's face toward' or 'to direct oneself toward.' سوی is the preposition 'toward,' سام is the proper name, نیرم here is an epithet (literally 'soft/tender') modifying Sam, نهادند is past tense plural 'they set/placed/turned,' and روی is 'face' used in the idiom.
segments.s068-g36: This is a nominal phrase: ابا ژنده‌پیلان is a compound/name (here treated as a group/name) and پرخاش‌جوی is an adjective meaning 'aggressive' or 'belligerent' describing them. Grammatically it is a noun phrase giving the identity/character of those who act.
segments.s068-g37: چو is the conjunction 'when.' زین کار is 'this deed/affair' (زین is an archaic/demonstrative 'this' and کار = 'deed, affair'). سام یل is apposition: 'Sam, the hero' (یل = 'hero'), and آگاه شد is the past construction 'became aware' (آگاه = 'aware', شد = past auxiliary 'became').
segments.s068-g38: پذیره here functions as 'reception/welcome' used elliptically to indicate that someone was welcomed/received (context supplies the subject). سوی marks direction/toward; پور کی functions as a name/compound (پور can mean 'son' or appear in names) and شاه شد is a simple past phrase 'became king.' In short: '(He/they) welcomed (him) and (he) became king (Pour‑Ki).' Note: کی here behaves as part of a name/title rather than the interrogative 'who.'
segments.s068-g39: ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from before' or 'previously/forward.' پدر نوذر is an appositive noun phrase 'father Nowzar' (پدر + proper name), and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying the noun phrase.
segments.s068-g40: بیامد is simple past 'came.' به نزدیک is a prepositional combination 'to/near' indicating proximity; سام is the proper name (the person toward whom he came), and سوار here indicates 'mounted/on horseback' (either a noun 'rider' or an adjectival sense 'mounted'). The sequence means 'came (forward) on horseback up close to Sam.'
segments.s068-g41: همه نامداران is the subject: همه (all/everyone) + نامداران (the renowned ones). پذیره شدند is a past-state expression: پذیره (reception/accepted) used adjectivally or as a participial noun and شدند (past plural of شدن) marks the resultant state 'became / were received / were welcomed.' Word order (Subject + predicate) and the compound predicative use are typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s068-g42: ابا here functions as an intransitive/semantically stative verb 'to balk / to be reluctant' describing the same subject (they). The two following items (ژنده‌پیل و تبیره) are predicates joined by و and describe the state they assumed; شدند (past plural of شدن) again marks 'they became' that way. Note both ژنده‌پیل (a figurative, compound epithet) and تبیره are poetic/archaic descriptive nouns/adjectives; the whole segment is a chained predicate referring back to همه نامداران.
segments.s068-g43: رسیدند is the simple past plural 'they arrived.' پس is an adverb 'then/after' (placed post-verb here in poetic word order). پیش سام سوار is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before Sam the rider' (پیش governing the following NP). The overall order is verb + temporal adverb + locative/prepositional phrase, a flexible order common in classical Persian.
segments.s068-g44: This is a coordinated noun phrase: بزرگان 'the great ones' and کی نوذر نامدار. کی here functions as a royal/title element before the name (a classical epic usage, not the interrogative 'who'), نوذر is the proper name, and نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned.' Note adjectives commonly follow the noun in older Persian.
segments.s068-g45: پیام is the direct object 'message.' پدر شاه نوذر is a genitive chain 'the father of King Nowzar' (possessor phrase). بداد is an archaic past verb here meaning 'gave / delivered' (verb placed after the noun phrase in this SOV-style line). The line therefore means 'they delivered the message of Nowzar's father.'
segments.s068-g46: به دیدار او is an adverbial phrase 'at/for the meeting with him' or 'on seeing him.' سام is the subject; یل is an appositive/title 'hero' attached to سام. گشت is past 'became/turned' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'joyful/happy.' The clause literally: 'Sam, the hero, became happy at meeting him.'
segments.s068-g47: چنین داد پاسخ is a stock formula 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). که introduces reported speech/clause. فرمان کنم is a first-person present/subjunctive construction 'I will (do) فرمان' — in classical usage فرمان کردن can mean either 'to command' or 'to obey' depending on context; here it reads naturally as 'I will comply/obey' (i.e., I will conform to the command or request). کنم is the present/subjunctive first-person form of کردن.
segments.s068-g48: ز دیدار او is 'from / by the sight of him' (ز = from/because of; دیدار او = seeing him / his sight). رامش جان is a two-word noun phrase 'the solace/peace of (my) soul' (رامش = solace/tranquillity; جان = soul/life), and کنم repeats the first-person verb 'I will make / I will find.' Together the clause expresses 'the sight of him will be the comfort of my soul' or 'I shall find solace of soul in seeing him.'
segments.s068-g137: This noun string lists paired terrain features in inverted/poetic order without the ezafe: نشیب و فراز = 'slopes (descents) and heights (ascents)'; بیابان و کوه = 'plain/desert and mountain.' Word order is simple coordination; no verb here — it sets the scene (location) for the following clause.
segments.s068-g138: به هر سو = 'to/toward every side' (here idiomatically 'in every direction'). شده is the perfect/resultant participle 'have become' (they have come to be); مردمان is the subject 'people'; هم‌گروه is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'of the same company / gathered together.' Together the clause says that, in all directions, people had become gathered in companies.
segments.s068-g139: سوار و پیاده = 'horsemen (riders) and foot-soldiers' (coordinated nouns). ده و دو هزار is an archaic numeric order meaning 'ten and two thousand,' i.e. twelve thousand; Persian poetic phrasing often lists the small number first but intends the total (here '۱۲٬۰۰۰').
segments.s068-g140: فگنده is a past participle/adjective from a verb meaning 'thrown/put forth' (here describing the troops as 'hurled out' or 'drawn up'); پدید آمد = 'appeared, came into view' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'); اندر شمار = a frozen phrase 'in the number / among the ranks' (اندر = 'in/within', شمار = 'number/count'). The whole phrase says the (12,000) appeared and were counted in the muster.
segments.s068-g141: چو + past verb بشنید = 'when he heard.' گفتار = 'speech/words.' سالار is 'commander/chief.' شاه here is the subject 'the king' (poetic omission of the ezafe/genitive links is normal), so the phrase reads 'when the king heard the commander’s words.'
segments.s068-g142: برافراخت is a past transitive 'he hoisted/raised up.' تا here links the verb to the following descriptive phrase, functioning as a poetic result/comparison marker ('and so ...' / 'raising up ...'); ماه فرخ کلاه is a compound epithet modifying کلاه — literally 'moon, fortunate, cap' — i.e. 'his fortunate moon-like cap' or 'the lucky cap like a moon.'
segments.s068-g143: چو + آمد = 'when ... came': چو روز از شب آمد = 'when the day came out of the night' (a standard temporal clause). بکوشش is an adverbial/ablative-like form from کوشیدن meaning 'with exertion / from effort' and ستوه is an adjective 'weary,' so the sequence qualifies the subject as 'weary from his toil' at the moment day arrived.
segments.s068-g144: ستوهی گرفته = a short adjectival clause 'seized/taken by weariness' (ستوهی = 'weary', گرفته = 'seized/struck'); فرو شد به کوه = 'he went down / descended into the mountain' (فرو = 'down', شد = past 'became/went', به = 'into/toward', کوه = 'mountain'). The whole segment states that, overcome with fatigue, he withdrew down into the mountain.
segments.s068-g25: همان کن is an imperative phrase: همان (demonstrative 'that') followed by the imperative کن ('do'). کجا here introduces the clause that follows (classical/poetic use close to 'that/which' or 'how/where'); با خرد is a prepositional phrase 'with wisdom' (با + noun). درخورد is an adjectival/predicative word meaning 'befitting/suitable' and qualifies the action just ordered — the whole segment = 'do that which, with wisdom, is fitting.'
segments.s068-g26: دل اژدها را is a definite object phrase: دل (heart) + modifier اژدها (of the dragon) + object marker را. خُرد is the subject ('wisdom') and بشکرد is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'to break/shatter' (optative/subjunctive sense here: 'let/may ... break'). Word order is poetic but essentially: 'Let wisdom break the dragon's heart.'
segments.s068-g27: بفرمود تا is a reporting + subordinate marker: بفرمود (honorific verb 'he said/ordered') + تا introducing the subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). نوذر is the named person (Nowzar). آمدش combines آمد (came/come) with enclitic -ش ('to him' / 'to it'), and پیش ('before, in the presence of') completes the clause: 'he ordered that Nowzar come to him before ...'.
segments.s068-g28: This is the noun phrase naming the addressees: ابا ویژگان is treated here as a proper name/title (in the text a compound name/epithet), و is 'and', بزرگان 'the great ones/nobles'. خوی + ش is the possessive construction 'خویش' = 'his own' (خوی 'kinship/own' + enclitic ش 'his'). Overall: 'before Aba Vīžegān and his great men.'
segments.s068-g29: بدو گفت is a common classical pattern meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto it as a prepositional/pronominal form; گفت introduces the reported speech). رو is an imperative 'go' (here treated as motion toward), followed by پیش + سام سوار indicating the destination/person in whose presence: 'go before Sam the rider.' The order mirrors spoken commands introduced by گفت.
segments.s068-g30: بپرسش is an imperative 'ask!' (classical form with enclitic -ش = 'him') commanding the listener to question. که introduces the content clause (indirect question), and چون آمد از کارزار is the indirect question 'how he came back from the battle' (چون = 'how', آمد = 'came', از کارزار = 'from the battle').
segments.s068-g31: چو دیدی is a temporal clause 'when you see (him)' (چو = 'when', دیدی = 'you see' / 'you will see' in classical usage). بگویش is an imperative/stative 'tell him' (بگو + -ش 'to him'). کزین‌سو is a directional phrase 'from this side' and گرای is an imperative/verb-form meaning 'turn' or 'change course' (poetic/older verb form). Together: 'When you see him, tell him: turn away from here.'
segments.s068-g32: This continues the command: ز نزدیک ما ('from near us' or 'close to us' — ز is the poetic preposition 'from' governing نزدیک ما), کن is an imperative 'make/do' used causatively ('cause him to...'), سوی خانه رای is a direction/purpose phrase: سوی = 'toward', خانه = 'house', رای = 'counsel/plan' (i.e. 'the house of counsel' or 'to the house for counsel'). The whole segment instructs the addressee to cause the man to come near them and go toward the house where counsel is given.
segments.s068-g1: پس (then) begins the clause as a temporal/conclusive particle; آگاهی (information/news) is the grammatical subject and آمد (came) is the past tense verb placed after the subject as is common in Persian narrative style. به شاه بزرگ (to the great king) is the prepositional indirect object headed by به + noun, with بزرگ as a postpositive adjective qualifying شاه.
segments.s068-g2: ز introduces the source/subject-matter of the news ('concerning/from'); the coordinate phrase مهراب و دستان سام سترگ is the content of that news. دستان here functions as a plural noun linked by و to مهراب; syntactically the whole phrase is a genitival/oblique complement of ز.
segments.s068-g3: ز پیوند مهراب is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/on account of the bond/union with Mehrab' (پیوند = bond/union). وز is a poetic/conjunctive link to the next phrase; مهر here is 'love/affection' and زال is the proper name Zal — together 'and from the love/affection of Zal'.
segments.s068-g4: وزان is a compound/poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from that' or 'by that means'; ناهمالان is the substantive being described (here contextually 'those formerly unequal/unmatched'), گشته is the perfect/passive participle meaning 'have become' or 'turned into', and همال is the resulting state 'peers/equals'. The verbal participle follows the noun phrase it modifies, yielding 'have become equals.'
segments.s068-g5: سخن رفت is an impersonal/implicative idiom: سخن (speech/news) + رفت (went) indicates that talk spread or was spoken. هر گونه ('every kind/any manner') qualifies the manner of that talk, and با موبدان ('with the mobeds') marks the interlocutors; word order places the verb رفت after its subject, typical of narrative Persian.
segments.s068-g6: به پیش ('in the presence of') is a locative/prepositional phrase headed by به + پیش, followed by the epithet سرافراز ('honoured/illustrious') modifying شاه (king). ردان is an epithet or name attached after شاه; the phrase as a whole marks the scene where the talk occurred.
segments.s068-g7: چنین introduces reported speech ('thus'); گفت is the speech verb 'said/spoke' (here used transitively with با + مخاطب). با بخردان ('with the wise ones') names the addressees; بخردان is a plural/collective form meaning 'the wise', and شهریار is the title 'sovereign/king' — the speaker role is therefore the king addressing the wise.
segments.s068-g8: که introduces an indirect question/that-clause ('what ...'); بر ما شود is a light-verb construction meaning 'befall us' or 'happen to us' (بر = upon/on, ما = us, شود = happen/become). زین is a deictic/poetic demonstrative here ('this/these') qualifying دژم روزگار ('grim/gloomy times'), where دژم modifies روزگار; the clause asks what will happen to us in these harsh times.
segments.s068-g169: سوی خانه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the house' (سوی = toward + خانه = house). بنهاد is the past form of بنهادن (an older/poetic variant of نهادن/نِهَدن) meaning 'set' or 'put' and here appears in the idiom سر بنهادن ('to set/turn one's head' = 'to head toward; set off for'). سر is the direct object in that idiom ('head' used idiomatically for 'direction/oneself'). با سپاه ('with an army') is an accompanying prepositional phrase modifying the action. Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + verb + object + accompaniment.
segments.s068-g170: بدان is an archaic/poetic compound (essentially 'به آن' / 'thereby' or 'by that') introducing the result; here it should be read together with باد. باد is the classical optative/formulaic particle ('may it be') which in epic narrative often functions as a fixed concluding/resultive marker ('thus was/so came'). پایان جوینده راه literally means 'the end of the seeker of the road' (جوینده modifies راه; the genitive relation is expressed without a modern ezāfe in this poetic order). Together the phrase states the outcome: 'thus came the end of the way-seeker.'
segments.s068-g49: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'to place/lay' (they placed/arranged); here it governs خوان ('the banquet/table') with verb before object, a poetic word order. و links two coordinated past verbs; گرفتند is 3rd-person plural past of گرفتن 'to take' and is followed by its object جام (postverbal object).
segments.s068-g50: نخست 'first' introduces the sequence. از is the preposition 'from/of' and takes the following name as its object; منوچهر is the proper name. بردند is 3rd-person plural past of بردن 'to take/carry' (they took/uttered), and نام 'name' is the direct object — here meaning 'the name of Manuchehr' (i.e. his name was taken/uttered first).
segments.s068-g51: پس از functions together as a temporal preposition 'after' (پس here has the 'after' sense and is followed by از). The list نوذر و سام و هر مهتری enumerates Nowzar, Sam and 'هر مهتری' ('every/each chief/elder'), with و as the coordinative 'and' and هر quantifying مهتری.
segments.s068-g52: گرفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they took/received' and governs شادی 'joy' as its object. ز is the poetic form of از 'from' marking origin, and هر کشوری means 'every country/realm' (هر modifies کشوری). The clause means they received/experienced joy from every land.
segments.s068-g53: به شادی درآمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'entered into joy / became a celebration' (به + noun + درآمد, where درآمد here has the sense of 'entering' or 'beginning'); شب دیریاز is the subject phrase 'the long-standing/long night' (noun شب followed by the adjective/epithet دیریاز). The verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion): 'Into joy there came a long night.'
segments.s068-g54: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. خورشید 'the sun' is modified by the adjective رخشنده 'radiant'. بگشاد is a poetic past form 'it opened/revealed' (ب‑ prefix + گشاد); راز 'secret' is the direct object. Together: 'when the radiant sun opened/revealed the secret.'
segments.s068-g55: خروش 'uproar/cry' is the noun subject and تبیره is a descriptive epithet used poetically (here left as a fixed epithet attached to خروش). برآمد is the 3rd-person past 'arose/issued forth'. ز در ('from the gate') uses the poetic z for 'from' and در in the sense 'gate/door'.
segments.s068-g56: هیون دلاور is a nominal phrase 'the beast/monster, valiant' (هیون 'beast' + دلاور 'brave/valiant' as epithet). برآورد is a past-form verb here meaning 'brought forth / raised' (3rd-person past in poetic diction), and پر 'feather/wing' is its object — 'it raised/lifted a wing'.
segments.s068-g145: می‌و (literally “mey-o” here) names the intoxicant placed before the gathering; it precedes مجلس and functions as the material/instrument introduced into the scene. آراست is the simple past (3rd sg.) of آراستن “to adorn/embellish”; the sequence می‌و مجلس آراست = “(he/they) adorned the assembly with wine.” و شد شادمان links a second result clause: شد is past of شدن “to become,” and شادمان is a predicative adjective meaning “joyful” placed after the verb (inversion common in poetry).
segments.s068-g146: جهان پاک is the nominal subject “the pure world.” دید is the simple past (3rd sg.) of دیدن “to see/behold.” The phrase از بد بدگمان is an ablative/ablative-like phrase introduced by از (“from/of”), with بد modifying the following nominal بدگمان (“ill suspicion / distrust”); the clause says that the pure world ‘saw’ (them) as being free of bad suspicion (the object/pronoun is omitted, as is common in Persian).
segments.s068-g147: به here marks the means or circumstance (“by/at”), introducing بگماز, a word from the verb-root meaning to appoint/command and functioning as the instrumental/agency notion “by order/command.” کوتاه کردند is the past plural (they made/caused to be short) with کوتاه as the adjective “short”; شب is the direct object placed after the verb: “they made the night short.”
segments.s068-g148: به یاد is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in memory/remembrance of” (به + یاد). سپهبد is the title “spahbed” (a senior commander). گشادند is the past plural of گشادن “to open” (here transitive), and لب is the object “lip(s)” — گشادند لب = “they opened their lips,” i.e. they began to speak, prompted by remembrance of the spahbed.
segments.s068-g149: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” شب روز شد is a small clause where شب is the subject and روز شد is the predicate composed of روز “day” plus شد (past of شدن) meaning “became day.” پرده بارگاه is a noun phrase “the curtain of the court/palace” placed after the temporal clause and picked up by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s068-g150: گشادند is past plural “they opened” (from گشودن/گشادن). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” دادند is past plural of دادن “they gave,” here meaning something like “they granted/made way.” زی is the preposition “from/of,” and شاه‌راه is the compound “royal/highway”; together دادند زی شاه‌راه expresses that they opened and gave passage or access to the royal road.
segments.s068-g151: بیامد is the simple past “came.” سپهدار is the title “spahbed/commander” functioning as an appositive to the proper name that follows. سام is the proper name and سترگ is an epithet attached to Sam (an older/poetic adjective meaning ‘mighty/great’ here; note that poetic diction sometimes uses archaic forms), so the chunk names the arriving figure: “the spahbed Sam the mighty came.”
segments.s068-g152: به نزد is the prepositional phrase “to / into the presence of” (به + نزد). منوچهر is the king’s name, شاه is the title “king,” and بزرگ is an epithet “great/elder”; the whole segment indicates the addressee/location: “to Manuchehr, the great king.”
segments.s068-g153: چنین (thus) modifies the verb گفت (said). The verb precedes the person spoken to: گفت با سام means “said to Sām,” with با marking the indirect object. شاه جهان is a title appositive identifying the speaker (“the king of the world”) and follows the indirect-object phrase by inversion (typical of poetic word order).
segments.s068-g154: کز introduces a reported clause (“that / that from here …”). ایدر is an archaic/poetic word for “here/from here.” برو is an imperative (“go!”). با plus the noun phrase گزیده مهان means “with the chosen (گزیده) guests (مهان)” — گزیده functions as an adjective/epithet, مهان = guests/companions.
segments.s068-g155: به هندوستان is a directional prepositional phrase “to / toward India.” اندر is an older/poetic preposition meaning “in, within,” and فروز here is an imperative verb (furoz) meaning “kindle / set alight / illuminate.” The order places the destination first and the verb phrase after it (poetic inversion).
segments.s068-g156: همه quantifies the following nouns (“all”). The noun phrase کاخ مهراب و کابل lists the direct objects to be destroyed: palaces (کاخ), the mihrab/sanctuary (مهراب), and Kabul (کابل). بسوز is an imperative verb (“burn!”). The verb comes at the end in the expected Persian command form.
segments.s068-g157: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction meaning “it must not be that / he should not.” او is the pronoun “he.” یابد is the verb “find / obtain” (here with a hortative/subjunctive flavor possible because of نباید که). از بد رها literal order: از (from) بد (evil/bad) رها (release/freedom) — “find release from evil.”
segments.s068-g158: که here continues/explains the previous clause (“for / that”). او ماند uses ماند (from ماندن) in the sense “will remain / will be left.” از بچه اژدها is a genitival phrase “of the dragon’s offspring” (بچه = offspring/young). The clause states the consequence: he will remain as / among the dragon’s brood.
segments.s068-g159: زمان تا زمان is an idiomatic/time phrase (“time to time / when the time comes”). زو is a poetic contraction of از او (“from him”), indicating source; here it precedes the verb. برآید is a verbal form meaning “arise / come forth.” خروش (roar/uproar) is the noun that rises — the clause therefore means “from time to time a roar will arise from him.” Note the poetic inversion (noun and verb order).
segments.s068-g160: شود is the intransitive verb “becomes / will be” (future/optative sense here). رام گیتی is an adjectival + noun phrase where رام (tame/gentle) qualifies گیتی (the world). پر از is a fixed expression meaning “full of,” followed by the coordinated nouns جنگ و جوش (“war and tumult / surge”): the world becomes filled with war and upheaval.
segments.s068-g161: هر is the universal determiner 'every/any'. آنکس is the indefinite/relative pronoun 'person/who' and is the head of the noun phrase; که introduces a relative clause modifying آنکس. پیوسته is an adverb/adjectival idea 'constantly/continually' that qualifies the following predicate; او is the third‑person pronoun 'him' (referring to the leader) and بود is the past tense 'was' (third person). The clause literally reads 'every person who constantly was his,' i.e. 'who was continually with him.'
segments.s068-g162: بزرگان is the plural noun 'great ones/major men' (subject). که again introduces a relative clause 'who…'. در + دسته form the locative phrase 'in the group/retinue,' with او as the possessive pronoun 'his' attached after دسته, and بود is again the past 'was/were.' The whole relative clause 'که در دسته او بود' modifies بزرگان: 'the great men who were in his band.'
segments.s068-g163: This line contains two consecutive imperatives. سر از تن جدا کن is an imperative clause: literal order 'head from body apart do' where جدا is 'apart/away' and کن is the imperative 'do' (i.e. 'separate'). The second imperative is زمین را بشوی where زمین 'the earth' is marked by the object marker را and بشوی is the imperative/subjunctive form 'wash' (second person). The two commands are coordinated by juxtaposition (no overt و).
segments.s068-g164: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning 'from.' پیوند here means 'bond/link' (the tie or graft), ضحاک is the proper name Zahhak, و is the conjunction 'and,' خویشان means 'relatives,' and اوی is the older/enclitic possessive form 'his' (attached to خویشان: 'his relatives'). The whole phrase modifies زمین را بشوی: 'wash the land from the bond of Zahhak and his kin.'
segments.s068-g165: چنین 'thus/so' qualifies the manner; داد (past of دادن) 'gave' combines with پاسخ 'answer' to mean 'gave such an answer.' The following که introduces the reported clause. ایدون is an archaic/poetic temporal/adverbial element (here 'now/at this time') and کنм is the first‑person present/subjunctive 'I should do / shall I do' used to pose 'what shall I do'.
segments.s068-g166: The second که again introduces the content of the question: کین 'hatred/vengeance' with از 'from' and دل 'heart' of شاه 'the king'. بیرون is the adverb 'out/away' and کنм is the 1st‑person verb 'I should remove/do' (subjunctive/optative sense). The clause expresses the goal/action: 'that I remove this hatred from the king's heart.'
segments.s068-g167: ببوسید is the past tense 'he kissed' (classical narrative past). تخت is the direct object 'throne.' و 'and' links the next action; بمالید is another past tense 'he rubbed/anointed' and روی here is the object 'the face' (the surface being anointed). The verbs precede their objects in this poetic narrative order.
segments.s068-g168: بران is the archaic contraction of بر آن meaning 'upon that/thereon.' نامور modifies مهر: 'renowned/famed seal.' مهر here is used in the sense 'seal/imprint' (not 'love'), انگشت is 'finger,' and اوی is the possessive form 'his.' The noun phrase thus reads 'the famed seal of his finger' (i.e. the king's signet impression) which he applies to the throne's face.
